examples.explanation: Step 1: Generate all binary tree structures with 3 nodes. Possible structures: (root with left child only, root with right child only, balanced with both children, etc.). There are 5 Catalan structures. Step 2: Assign values 1,2,3 in inorder to each structure. For example, structure with root and left child: assign 1 to left, 2 to root, 3 to right (if exists). Step 3: Filter duplicates by comparing trees. All 5 structures yield unique BSTs when values assigned. Final count: 5.

Step 1: Generate structures with 2 nodes. Structures: root with left child, root with right child (2 structures). Step 2: Assign values 1,2 in inorder. For left-child structure: left=1, root=2. For right-child structure: root=1, right=2. Step 3: No duplicates, both are unique. Final count: 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(4^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible binary tree structures with n nodes, then filter out duplicates by checking structural and value uniqueness. This approach explores all tree configurations without leveraging BST properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique BSTs with n Nodes Using Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Generate all possible binary tree structures with n nodes, then filter duplicates by checking structural and value uniqueness.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by generating all possible binary tree structures with n nodes, then filtering out duplicates. The intuition is that any binary tree with n nodes can be represented by its structure and node values (1 to n). We generate all possible structures recursively by considering each node as a root and combining left and right subtrees. Since we're not leveraging BST properties, we must check for duplicates by comparing tree structures and node values. The algorithm: 1. Generate all possible binary trees with n nodes using recursion. For each root value i from 1 to n, generate all left subtrees with i-1 nodes and right subtrees with n-i nodes. Combine them. 2. For each generated tree, assign values 1 to n in inorder traversal to maintain BST property (though we're not using BST logic, this ensures unique value assignment). 3. Filter duplicates by comparing tree structures and values. Time complexity is O(4^n) due to Catalan number growth of binary trees, and space is O(4^n) for storing all trees. This feeds from L1 by using the tree generation subroutine and adding duplicate filtering. Common mistakes include not handling empty subtrees correctly or inefficient duplicate checking. Use recursion with memoization for structure generation and tree comparison for filtering.

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate all binary tree structures with 3 nodes. Possible structures: (root with left child only, root with right child only, balanced with both children, etc.). There are 5 Catalan structures. Step 2: Assign values 1,2,3 in inorder to each structure. For example, structure with root and left child: assign 1 to left, 2 to root, 3 to right (if exists). Step 3: Filter duplicates by comparing trees. All 5 structures yield unique BSTs when values assigned. Final count: 5.

Step 1: Generate structures with 2 nodes. Structures: root with left child, root with right child (2 structures). Step 2: Assign values 1,2 in inorder. For left-child structure: left=1, root=2. For right-child structure: root=1, right=2. Step 3: No duplicates, both are unique. Final count: 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Binary Tree Structures with n Nodes
ladders.desc: Recursively generate all possible binary tree structures without considering node values.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine generates all possible binary tree structures for a given number of nodes n. The intuition is that any binary tree can be built by choosing a root and combining all possible left and right subtrees. The algorithm: 1. Base cases: if n=0, return a list containing None (empty tree). If n=1, return a list with a single node structure. 2. For n>1, iterate i from 0 to n-1 for left subtree nodes. Generate all left structures with i nodes and right structures with n-1-i nodes. 3. For each pair of left and right structures, create a new tree with a root node connecting them. Collect all combinations. Time complexity is O(4^n) due to Catalan number C_n, and space is O(4^n) for storing structures. This feeds into L0 by providing the structural basis for value assignment. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in node counting or missing empty subtree cases. Use recursion with memoization to avoid recomputation.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=2, iterate i=0 to 1. i=0: left structures for 0 nodes = [None], right for 1 node = [single node]. Combine: root with left=None, right=single node → structure A. i=1: left for 1 node = [single node], right for 0 nodes = [None]. Combine: root with left=single node, right=None → structure B. Total: 2 structures.

Step 1: n=3, iterate i=0 to 2. i=0: left=None, right structures for 2 nodes (2 structures) → 2 combinations. i=1: left structures for 1 node (1 structure), right for 1 node (1 structure) → 1 combination. i=2: left for 2 nodes (2 structures), right=None → 2 combinations. Total: 2+1+2=5 structures.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Tree Generation Using Catalan Number Pattern
ladders.desc: Use recursion to generate trees by partitioning nodes between left and right subtrees.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves recursively generating trees by dividing n nodes into left and right subtrees. The intuition is that the number of binary trees with n nodes follows the Catalan number sequence, which can be computed recursively: C_n = sum_{i=0}^{n-1} C_i * C_{n-1-i}. This mirrors the tree generation: for a tree with n nodes, choose a root (1 node), then distribute remaining n-1 nodes between left and right subtrees. The algorithm: 1. Define function generate(n): if n=0 return [None]. 2. Initialize result list. 3. For i from 0 to n-1: left_trees = generate(i), right_trees = generate(n-1-i). 4. For each left in left_trees and right in right_trees: create new tree with root, left, right, add to result. 5. Return result. Time complexity is O(4^n) due to exponential recursion, space is O(4^n) for storage. This feeds into L1 as the implementation of structure generation. Common mistakes include not caching results for efficiency or incorrect base cases. Use memoization to store results for each n.

examples.trace: Step 1: generate(2). i=0: left=generate(0)=[None], right=generate(1)=[single node]. Combine: root with left=None, right=single node → tree1. i=1: left=generate(1)=[single node], right=generate(0)=[None]. Combine: root with left=single node, right=None → tree2. Result: [tree1, tree2], count=2.

Step 1: generate(3). i=0: left=[None], right=generate(2)=[tree1, tree2] → 2 trees. i=1: left=generate(1)=[single node], right=generate(1)=[single node] → 1 tree. i=2: left=generate(2)=[tree1, tree2], right=[None] → 2 trees. Total: 2+1+2=5 trees.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Combination of Subtrees
ladders.desc: Combine left and right subtree lists to form new trees.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on combining lists of left and right subtrees to create new trees. The intuition is that for a given root, we need to attach every possible left subtree with every possible right subtree to generate all configurations. The algorithm: 1. Given two lists: left_trees and right_trees. 2. Initialize an empty result list. 3. Nested loops: for each left in left_trees and each right in right_trees: create a new tree node with left and right as children, add to result. 4. If left_trees or right_trees is empty (contains only None), handle it appropriately. Time complexity is O(L*R) where L and R are sizes of left and right lists, space is O(L*R) for the new trees. This feeds into L2 as the step where subtrees are combined in the recursion. Common mistakes include not handling empty subtrees or creating duplicate references. Ensure new node creation for each combination.

examples.trace: Step 1: left_trees has 1 element (None), right_trees has 1 element (Node). Step 2: Loop: left=None, right=Node. Create new tree: root with left=None, right=Node. Add to result. Result list has 1 tree.

examples.output: [Tree with root, left=Node1, right=Node2], [Tree with root, left=Node1, right=Node3]
examples.trace: Step 1: left_trees has 1 element (Node1), right_trees has 2 elements (Node2, Node3). Step 2: First iteration: left=Node1, right=Node2 → create tree A. Second iteration: left=Node1, right=Node3 → create tree B. Result: [tree A, tree B].

testCases.expected: [root with left=Node1, right=Node3], [root with left=Node2, right=Node3]
[root with left=Node1, right=Node2], [root with left=Node1, right=Node3]
[root with left=None, right=Node], [root with left=Node, right=Node]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Create a Binary Tree Node with Left and Right Children
ladders.desc: Define and instantiate a tree node structure.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating a binary tree node, which is the fundamental building block for trees. The intuition is that a binary tree is composed of nodes, each with a value and references to left and right children. The algorithm: 1. Define a class or structure for a tree node with attributes: value, left, right. 2. Initialize a node with given value, and set left and right to provided children (could be None or other nodes). 3. In pseudocode: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. Time complexity is O(1) per node creation, space is O(1) per node. This feeds into L3 as the node creation step when combining subtrees. Common mistakes include not initializing attributes correctly or sharing references incorrectly. Always create new node instances for each tree.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call TreeNode(1, None, None). Step 2: Create new object, set val=1, left=None, right=None. Result: a single node with no children.

Step 1: Create left child: TreeNode(1) → node A. Step 2: Create right child: TreeNode(3) → node B. Step 3: Create root: TreeNode(2, node A, node B). Result: root node with left child val=1 and right child val=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Trees and Recursion
ladders.desc: Learn basic binary tree structure and simple recursive functions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers binary trees and recursion, essential for the problem. A binary tree is a hierarchical structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller subproblems. Intuition: Binary trees are naturally recursive—a tree consists of a root and subtrees (left and right), which are themselves trees. For recursion, define base cases (e.g., empty tree) and recursive cases (e.g., process root, then recurse on children). Algorithm for a simple recursive function: 1. Define function f(n): if n==0 return 1 (base case). Else return n * f(n-1) (recursive case). For trees, example: count nodes: if root is None return 0; else return 1 + count(left) + count(right). Time/space depend on tree depth. This feeds into L4 as the basis for tree operations. Common mistakes: missing base cases or infinite recursion. Practice with simple examples.

examples.input: Binary tree with root 1, left 2, right 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Root node has value 1. Step 2: Left child is node with value 2, right child is node with value 3. Step 3: This forms a tree: 1 is parent, 2 and 3 are children. Visualize as: 1 / \ 2 3.

examples.input: Recursive function to compute factorial of 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Call fact(3). Since 3!=0, return 3 * fact(2). Step 2: Call fact(2): return 2 * fact(1). Step 3: Call fact(1): return 1 * fact(0). Step 4: Call fact(0): return 1 (base case). Step 5: Back substitute: fact(1)=1*1=1, fact(2)=2*1=2, fact(3)=3*2=6.

testCases.expected: Tree with root 10 and left child 5
Tree with root 10 and right child 15

testCases.input: Recursive sum of numbers from 1 to 3
Count nodes in tree: root=1, left=2, right=3
Binary tree height for single node

approaches.name: Recursive DP with Memoization O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use recursion to generate all BSTs by selecting each node as root, recursively building left and right subtrees from appropriate value ranges, and memoizing results to avoid recomputation. This leverages the BST property that left subtree values are smaller than root.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique BSTs with Recursive DP and Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all structurally unique BSTs for values 1 to n using recursive DP with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires generating all structurally unique BSTs that can be formed using values from 1 to n. The key insight is that for any BST, if we choose a value i as the root, all values less than i must be in the left subtree, and all values greater than i must be in the right subtree. This property allows us to break the problem into smaller subproblems recursively. We use memoization to store results for subproblems defined by a range [start, end] to avoid recomputation. The algorithm works by iterating through each value i in the range as a potential root. For each i, we recursively generate all possible left subtrees from the range [start, i-1] and all possible right subtrees from [i+1, end]. Then, we combine each left subtree with each right subtree, attaching them to the root i to form a BST. The base case is when start > end, which returns a list containing a single null node (representing an empty subtree). The time complexity is O(n^2) because there are O(n^2) subproblems (each defined by start and end), and each subproblem takes O(n) time to combine results, but with memoization, each subproblem is solved once. The space complexity is O(n^2) for storing memoized results and recursion stack. This approach efficiently leverages the BST property and dynamic programming to avoid exponential recomputation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call generateTrees(1, 3).
Step 2: For i = 1 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 0) returns [null], right = generateTrees(2, 3).
  - For right: i = 2 as root: left = generateTrees(2, 1) returns [null], right = generateTrees(3, 3).
    * For right: i = 3 as root: left = generateTrees(3, 2) returns [null], right = generateTrees(4, 3) returns [null]. Combine: root 3 with left null and right null.
    * Combine for i=2: root 2 with left null and right subtree (root 3).
  - Also for right: i = 3 as root: left = generateTrees(2, 2).
    * For left: i = 2 as root: left = generateTrees(2, 1) returns [null], right = generateTrees(3, 2) returns [null]. Combine: root 2 with left null and right null.
    * Combine for i=3: root 3 with left subtree (root 2) and right null.
  - Combine for i=1: root 1 with left null and right subtrees (two options: root 2 with right 3, and root 3 with left 2).
Step 3: For i = 2 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 1), right = generateTrees(3, 3).
  - For left: i = 1 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 0) returns [null], right = generateTrees(2, 1) returns [null]. Combine: root 1 with left null and right null.
  - For right: i = 3 as root: left = generateTrees(3, 2) returns [null], right = generateTrees(4, 3) returns [null]. Combine: root 3 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=2: root 2 with left subtree (root 1) and right subtree (root 3).
Step 4: For i = 3 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 2), right = generateTrees(4, 3) returns [null].
  - For left: similar to step 2 right part, generates two subtrees: root 1 with right 2, and root 2 with left 1.
  - Combine for i=3: root 3 with left subtrees (two options) and right null.
Step 5: Total unique BSTs: 5 (as listed in output).

Step 1: Call generateTrees(1, 2).
Step 2: For i = 1 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 0) returns [null], right = generateTrees(2, 2).
  - For right: i = 2 as root: left = generateTrees(2, 1) returns [null], right = generateTrees(3, 2) returns [null]. Combine: root 2 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=1: root 1 with left null and right subtree (root 2).
Step 3: For i = 2 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 1), right = generateTrees(3, 2) returns [null].
  - For left: i = 1 as root: left = generateTrees(1, 0) returns [null], right = generateTrees(2, 1) returns [null]. Combine: root 1 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=2: root 2 with left subtree (root 1) and right null.
Step 4: Total unique BSTs: 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function to Generate BSTs for a Range
ladders.desc: The helper function that recursively generates all BSTs for a given range of values using memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that generates all unique BSTs for a contiguous range of integer values from 'start' to 'end'. It leverages the BST property: for any root value i in the range, the left subtree must contain values from start to i-1, and the right subtree from i+1 to end. The function uses memoization to store results for each (start, end) pair to avoid recomputation. The base case occurs when start > end, indicating an empty range, for which we return a list containing a null node (representing an empty tree). For each i from start to end, we recursively call the function for the left range (start, i-1) and right range (i+1, end). We then combine every left subtree with every right subtree, creating a new root node with value i, and attach the left and right subtrees. All such trees are collected in a list and returned. The time complexity for this function is O(n^2) due to the number of subproblems and combination steps, but memoization ensures each subproblem is solved once. Space complexity is O(n^2) for the memoization dictionary and recursion stack. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem by being called with start=1 and end=n.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Call function with start=1, end=2.
Step 2: For i=1: left = call(1,0) returns [null], right = call(2,2).
  - For right: i=2: left = call(2,1) returns [null], right = call(3,2) returns [null]. Combine: root 2 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=1: root 1 with left null and right subtree (root 2).
Step 3: For i=2: left = call(1,1), right = call(3,2) returns [null].
  - For left: i=1: left = call(1,0) returns [null], right = call(2,1) returns [null]. Combine: root 1 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=2: root 2 with left subtree (root 1) and right null.
Step 4: Return list containing two BSTs.

Step 1: Call function with start=2, end=3.
Step 2: For i=2: left = call(2,1) returns [null], right = call(3,3).
  - For right: i=3: left = call(3,2) returns [null], right = call(4,3) returns [null]. Combine: root 3 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=2: root 2 with left null and right subtree (root 3).
Step 3: For i=3: left = call(2,2), right = call(4,3) returns [null].
  - For left: i=2: left = call(2,1) returns [null], right = call(3,2) returns [null]. Combine: root 2 with left null and right null.
  - Combine for i=3: root 3 with left subtree (root 2) and right null.
Step 4: Return list containing two BSTs.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Breaking Problem into Subproblems Using BST Property
ladders.desc: The central technique of dividing the BST generation problem based on root selection and value ranges.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using the fundamental property of Binary Search Trees (BSTs): for any node, all values in the left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater. This property allows us to decompose the problem of generating all unique BSTs for a set of values into smaller, independent subproblems. Given a range of values from start to end, we consider each value i in that range as a potential root. Once i is chosen as root, the left subtree must be formed from values start to i-1, and the right subtree from i+1 to end. These left and right subtrees are themselves BSTs, so we can recursively generate them. The key insight is that the subproblems for left and right subtrees are independent and can be solved separately, then combined. This divide-and-conquer approach reduces the exponential problem into manageable parts. The intuition is similar to the Catalan number recurrence, where the number of BSTs for n nodes is the sum over i of (BSTs for i-1 nodes) * (BSTs for n-i nodes). By memoizing results for each (start, end) pair, we avoid recomputing the same subproblems, leading to efficient O(n^2) time. This logic is the heart of the recursive DP approach and feeds into the key sub-routine by defining how to split and combine subtrees.

examples.output: Left range: 1 to 1, Right range: 3 to 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: For range [1,3], choose root value i=2.
Step 2: Left subtree must contain values less than 2: range [1,1].
Step 3: Right subtree must contain values greater than 2: range [3,3].
Step 4: Recursively generate all BSTs for left range [1,1] (which gives one BST: root 1).
Step 5: Recursively generate all BSTs for right range [3,3] (which gives one BST: root 3).
Step 6: Combine: for each left BST and each right BST, create a root node 2 with left and right subtrees attached. Here, only one left and one right, so one combined BST.

examples.output: Left range: 1 to 2, Right range: 4 to 4
examples.trace:
Step 1: For range [1,4], choose root value i=3.
Step 2: Left subtree range: values less than 3, so [1,2].
Step 3: Right subtree range: values greater than 3, so [4,4].
Step 4: Recursively generate all BSTs for left range [1,2] (which gives 2 BSTs).
Step 5: Recursively generate all BSTs for right range [4,4] (which gives 1 BST: root 4).
Step 6: Combine: for each of the 2 left BSTs and the 1 right BST, create a root node 3 with left and right attached, resulting in 2 combined BSTs.

testCases.expected: Left range: 1 to 0, Right range: 2 to 1
Left range: 1 to 0, Right range: 2 to 2
Left range: 1 to 1, Right range: 3 to 2
Left range: 1 to 0, Right range: 2 to 3
Left range: 1 to 2, Right range: 4 to 3
Left range: 2 to 2, Right range: 4 to 4
Left range: 1 to 2, Right range: 4 to 5
Left range: 1 to 4, Right range: 6 to 5
Left range: 3 to 4, Right range: 6 to 7
Left range: 1 to 6, Right range: 8 to 10

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Combinations of Left and Right Subtrees
ladders.desc: The intermediate step of combining every possible left subtree with every possible right subtree for a given root.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves taking lists of possible left subtrees and right subtrees (generated recursively) and combining them to form all unique BSTs for a specific root value. For a chosen root value i, after recursively generating all BSTs for the left range (start to i-1) and right range (i+1 to end), we have two lists: left_trees and right_trees. The combination step requires creating a new BST for every pair (left_tree, right_tree) where left_tree is from left_trees and right_tree is from right_trees. This is done by iterating through each left_tree and each right_tree, creating a new tree node with value i, setting its left child to left_tree and right child to right_tree, and adding this tree to a result list. If left_trees or right_trees is empty (which happens when the range is invalid, e.g., start > end, represented as a list with a null node), we still iterate: for example, if left_trees has one null node and right_trees has one actual tree, we combine them to form a tree with root i, null left, and the right tree. This step ensures that all structural variations are captured. The time complexity for this combination is O(L * R) where L and R are the sizes of left_trees and right_trees, but in the overall algorithm, this contributes to the O(n^2) total. This building block is essential for the core logic, as it materializes the BSTs from the subdivided ranges.

examples.output: One BST: root 2 with left null and right subtree (root 3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: left_trees contains one element: null node.
Step 2: right_trees contains one element: a tree with root node 3 (and no children).
Step 3: Iterate over left_trees: for left_tree = null.
Step 4: Iterate over right_trees: for right_tree = tree(root=3).
Step 5: Create new node with value 2.
Step 6: Set left child of new node to null.
Step 7: Set right child of new node to tree(root=3).
Step 8: Add this new tree to result list.
Step 9: Result list has one BST.

examples.input: Root = 3, left_trees = [tree(root=1 with right=2), tree(root=2 with left=1)], right_trees = [null]
examples.output: Two BSTs: root 3 with two different left subtrees and null right
examples.trace:
Step 1: left_trees has two elements: BST1 (root 1 with right child 2) and BST2 (root 2 with left child 1).
Step 2: right_trees has one element: null node.
Step 3: For left_tree = BST1: iterate over right_trees: for right_tree = null. Create root 3 with left=BST1 and right=null. Add to result.
Step 4: For left_tree = BST2: iterate over right_trees: for right_tree = null. Create root 3 with left=BST2 and right=null. Add to result.
Step 5: Result list has two BSTs.

testCases.expected: One BST: root 1 with null left and right
One BST: root 2 with left subtree (root 1) and null right
One BST: root 3 with null left and right subtree (root 4)
One BST: root 4 with left subtree (root 2) and right subtree (root 5)
Two BSTs: root 5 with left subtree (root 3) and two different right subtrees
Two BSTs: root 6 with two different left subtrees and null right
Four BSTs: all combinations of left and right subtrees
Two BSTs: root 8 with null left and two different right subtrees
Two BSTs: root 9 with two different left subtrees and same right subtree

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Creating a Tree Node and Attaching Subtrees
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of constructing a BST node and linking left and right children.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating a new tree node and setting its left and right child pointers to form a BST. In the context of the problem, a tree node typically has three attributes: a value (integer), a left child (pointer to another node or null), and a right child (similarly a pointer or null). The operation is performed when combining subtrees: for a given root value i, a left subtree L, and a right subtree R, we instantiate a new node with value i, assign its left child to L, and assign its right child to R. This constructs a new BST where the root satisfies the BST property if L and R are valid BSTs (which they are, by recursive construction). In pseudocode, this might look like: node = TreeNode(i); node.left = L; node.right = R. It's crucial to handle cases where L or R are null (representing empty subtrees) by setting the pointers to null. This operation is atomic and is repeated many times during the combination step. The time complexity is O(1) per node creation, and space complexity is O(1) for the node object, but overall it contributes to the O(n^2) space for storing all trees. This operation feeds into the building block as the action taken for each pair of left and right subtrees.

examples.output: A tree node with value 5 and null children
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create a new TreeNode object.
Step 2: Set its value attribute to 5.
Step 3: Set its left attribute to null.
Step 4: Set its right attribute to null.
Step 5: The node is now a BST with root 5 and no subtrees.

examples.input: Value = 3, left = node with value 1, right = node with value 4
examples.output: A tree with root 3, left child 1, right child 4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create a new TreeNode with value 3.
Step 2: Set its left attribute to an existing TreeNode with value 1 (which has null children).
Step 3: Set its right attribute to an existing TreeNode with value 4 (which has null children).
Step 4: The resulting tree has root 3, left subtree rooted at 1, and right subtree rooted at 4.

Node with value 2, left child node(1), right null
Node with value 3, left null, right child node(4)
Node with value 4, left child node(2), right child node(5)
Node with value 5, left child node(3), right child node(6)
Node with value 6, left child node(4), right child node(7)
Node with value 7, left child node(5), right child node(8)
Node with value 8, left child node(6), right child node(9)
Node with value 9, left child node(7), right child node(10)
Node with value 10, left child node(8), right child node(11)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Binary Search Tree (BST) Structure
ladders.desc: The prerequisite concept of what a BST is and its fundamental property.
ladders.explanation: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree data structure where each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. The key property that defines a BST is that for any node, all values in its left subtree are strictly less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are strictly greater than the node's value. This property must hold for every node in the tree. For example, if a node has value 5, then its left subtree can only contain values like 1, 2, 3, or 4, and its right subtree can only contain values like 6, 7, or 8. This ordering property enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations, but in this problem, we focus on generating all possible tree structures that satisfy this property. The tree can be empty (null), which is trivially a BST. When we talk about 'unique BSTs', we mean structurally unique trees; for example, two trees with the same shape but different node values are considered different if the values are assigned differently, but in this problem, values are fixed from 1 to n, so structure uniqueness implies different arrangements of nodes. Understanding this property is foundational because it dictates how we can recursively build trees: by selecting a root and ensuring left and right subtrees adhere to the value ranges. This concept feeds into the basic operation, as attaching subtrees must maintain this property.

examples.output: Example valid BST: root 2 with left child 1 and right child 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Consider a tree with root node value 2.
Step 2: Left child is node with value 1. Check property: 1 < 2, so valid.
Step 3: Right child is node with value 3. Check property: 3 > 2, so valid.
Step 4: For node 1: it has no children, so property holds.
Step 5: For node 3: it has no children, so property holds.
Step 6: The entire tree satisfies BST property.

examples.output: Example invalid BST: root 1 with right child 3 and left child 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Consider a tree with root node value 1.
Step 2: Left child is node with value 2. Check property: 2 is not less than 1, so invalid.
Step 3: Since the property is violated at the root, the tree is not a BST, even if other parts might be valid.

Tree: root 5 with left child 3 and right null
Tree: root 5 with left null and right child 7
Tree: root 5 with left child 7 and right child 3

testCases.input: Tree: root 5 with left child 3 and right child 4
testCases.expected: Invalid BST (right child less than root)

testCases.input: Tree: root 5 with left child 3 (and left child of 3 is 6) and right null
testCases.expected: Invalid BST (6 in left subtree of 3 is greater than 5)

testCases.input: Tree: root 10 with left child 5 (and left of 5 is 3, right of 5 is 7) and right child 15
Tree: root 10 with left child 5 (and left of 5 is 12) and right child 15
testCases.expected: Invalid BST (12 in left subtree of 5 is greater than 10)

testCases.input: Tree: root 8 with left child 4 (and left of 4 is 2, right of 4 is 6) and right child 12 (and left of 12 is 10, right of 12 is 14)

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Bottom-Up O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Build solutions iteratively from smaller subproblems to larger ones, storing all unique BSTs for each number of nodes and combining them systematically. This avoids recursion overhead and builds results in a tabular manner.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Generate All Unique BSTs Using DP Bottom-Up
ladders.desc: Complete solution to generate all structurally unique BSTs with n nodes using dynamic programming with tabulation.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires generating all possible binary search trees (BSTs) that can be formed with n nodes labeled 1 to n. The DP bottom-up approach builds solutions iteratively by storing all unique BSTs for each number of nodes. The key insight is that for a given root value r, the left subtree must contain values 1 to r-1 (forming all possible BSTs with r-1 nodes) and the right subtree must contain values r+1 to n (forming all possible BSTs with n-r nodes). By combining every left subtree with every right subtree for each possible root, we generate all unique BSTs for n nodes. We start with the base case of 0 nodes (which is a single empty tree) and build up to n nodes. The algorithm uses a DP table where dp[i] stores all unique BSTs with i nodes. For each i from 1 to n, we iterate through all possible roots r from 1 to i. For each root r, we combine all BSTs from dp[r-1] (left subtrees) with all BSTs from dp[i-r] (right subtrees), adjusting node values in the right subtrees by adding r to maintain the BST property. The time complexity is O(n^2 * C_n) where C_n is the Catalan number (exponential), but practically O(n^2) for generating structures. Space complexity is O(n^2) for storing all trees. This approach avoids recursion overhead and builds results systematically, making it efficient for moderate n. Connection to L1: This level implements the full algorithm using the combination subroutine from L1. Common mistakes include forgetting to adjust node values in right subtrees, incorrectly handling empty trees, or inefficient tree copying. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[0] = [None]; for i in range(1, n+1): dp[i] = []; for r in range(1, i+1): for left in dp[r-1]: for right in dp[i-r]: root = TreeNode(r); root.left = left; root.right = clone_and_offset(right, r); dp[i].append(root); return dp[n].

examples.output: 5 unique BSTs (structures shown as root values with left/right)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[0] = [None]. Step 2: For i=1: r=1 only. left=dp[0]=[None], right=dp[0]=[None]. Create root=1 with left=None, right=None. dp[1]=[tree1]. Step 3: For i=2: r=1: left=dp[0]=[None], right=dp[1]=[tree1]. Adjust right by adding 1: tree1 becomes node 2. Create root=1 with left=None, right=2. r=2: left=dp[1]=[tree1], right=dp[0]=[None]. Create root=2 with left=1, right=None. dp[2]=[tree(1,null,2), tree(2,1,null)]. Step 4: For i=3: r=1: left=dp[0]=[None], right=dp[2]=[two trees]. Adjust right by adding 1: tree(1,null,2) becomes tree(2,null,3); tree(2,1,null) becomes tree(3,2,null). Create roots: 1 with left=None, right=tree(2,null,3) and 1 with left=None, right=tree(3,2,null). r=2: left=dp[1]=[tree1], right=dp[1]=[tree1]. Adjust right by adding 2: tree1 becomes node 3. Create root=2 with left=1, right=3. r=3: left=dp[2]=[two trees], right=dp[0]=[None]. Create roots: 3 with left=tree(1,null,2), right=None and 3 with left=tree(2,1,null), right=None. dp[3] has 5 trees total.

Step 1: dp[0]=[None]. Step 2: i=1: r=1, left=None, right=None, create tree(1). dp[1]=[tree(1)]. Step 3: i=2: r=1: left=None, right=dp[1]=[tree(1)], adjust right by adding 1: tree(1) becomes node 2, create tree(1,null,2). r=2: left=dp[1]=[tree(1)], right=None, create tree(2,1,null). dp[2]=[tree(1,null,2), tree(2,1,null)].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Combine Left and Right Subtrees for a Given Root
ladders.desc: Generate all BSTs for a specific root by combining all possible left and right subtrees.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the DP approach. Given a root value r and the number of total nodes i, we need to generate all BSTs where the root is r, the left subtree contains r-1 nodes (values 1 to r-1), and the right subtree contains i-r nodes (values r+1 to i). The intuition is that BSTs are uniquely determined by their structure and node values, and by fixing the root, we can independently combine all valid left and right subtrees. We retrieve all possible left subtrees from dp[r-1] (which are BSTs with values 1 to r-1) and all possible right subtrees from dp[i-r] (which are BSTs with values 1 to i-r). However, right subtrees must be adjusted by adding r to each node value to shift the range from 1..(i-r) to (r+1)..i, maintaining the BST property. For each pair (left, right), we create a new tree with root r, attach left as is, and attach a cloned and value-adjusted right subtree. This ensures all combinations are generated without duplication. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(L * R) where L and R are the number of trees in dp[r-1] and dp[i-r], which grows with Catalan numbers. Space complexity is O(L * R) for storing new trees. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each root in the main DP loop. Common mistakes include not cloning right subtrees (leading to shared references), incorrect value adjustment, or missing the base case of empty subtrees. Real code pattern: def combine_subtrees(r, left_trees, right_trees): result = []; for left in left_trees: for right in right_trees: root = TreeNode(r); root.left = left; adjusted_right = clone_and_offset(right, r); root.right = adjusted_right; result.append(root); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: left_trees has one tree: node 1. Step 2: right_trees has one tree: node 1. Step 3: Adjust right tree by adding r=2: node 1 becomes node 3. Step 4: Create root node 2 with left=node 1, right=node 3. Result is a single tree: root 2, left 1, right 3.

Step 1: left_trees is [None]. Step 2: right_trees has two trees: tree(1,null,2) and tree(2,1,null). Step 3: Adjust right trees by adding r=1: tree(1,null,2) becomes tree(2,null,3); tree(2,1,null) becomes tree(3,2,null). Step 4: For each right: Create root 1 with left=None, right=adjusted tree. Result: two trees: 1 with right=tree(2,null,3) and 1 with right=tree(3,2,null).

testCases.input: r=4, left_trees=[tree(1,null,2,null,3), tree(1,null,3,2,null), tree(2,1,3), tree(3,1,null,null,2), tree(3,2,null,1,null)], right_trees=[None]
testCases.expected: 5 trees with root 4 and left as each of the 5 trees

testCases.input: r=1, left_trees=[None], right_trees=[tree(1,null,2,null,3), tree(1,null,3,2,null), tree(2,1,3), tree(3,1,null,null,2), tree(3,2,null,1,null)]
testCases.expected: 5 trees with root 1 and right adjusted

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Table for Storing Unique BSTs per Node Count
ladders.desc: Build a table where each entry dp[i] contains all unique BSTs that can be formed with i nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is using dynamic programming with tabulation to store solutions to subproblems. We define dp as a list where dp[i] is a list of all unique BSTs with i nodes (values 1 to i). The intuition is that the number of unique BSTs for i nodes depends only on the number of nodes, not the specific values, because we can always relabel values from 1 to i while preserving structure. This allows us to build dp iteratively: start with dp[0] = [None] (the empty tree), and for each i from 1 to n, compute dp[i] by considering all possible roots r from 1 to i. For each r, the left subtree has r-1 nodes (so we use dp[r-1]) and the right subtree has i-r nodes (so we use dp[i-r], with values adjusted by adding r). By combining all left and right subtrees for each r, we generate all trees for dp[i]. This avoids recomputation and ensures all structures are captured. Time complexity for building dp up to n is O(n^2 * C_n) in theory, but practically O(n^2) for the loops, with Catalan growth in tree counts. Space complexity is O(n^2) for storing all trees. Connection to L1: This level sets up the DP table and calls the combination subroutine for each root. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors), not initializing dp[0] properly, or inefficient storage of trees. Real code pattern: dp = [[] for _ in range(n+1)]; dp[0] = [None]; for i in range(1, n+1): for r in range(1, i+1): left_trees = dp[r-1]; right_trees = dp[i-r]; combined = combine_subtrees(r, left_trees, right_trees); dp[i].extend(combined); return dp[n].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[0]=[None]. Step 2: i=1: r=1 only. left_trees=dp[0]=[None], right_trees=dp[0]=[None]. Combine: create tree(1). dp[1]=[tree(1)]. Step 3: i=2: r=1: left_trees=dp[0]=[None], right_trees=dp[1]=[tree(1)], adjust right by adding 1, combine: tree(1,null,2). r=2: left_trees=dp[1]=[tree(1)], right_trees=dp[0]=[None], combine: tree(2,1,null). dp[2]=[tree(1,null,2), tree(2,1,null)].

examples.output: dp[0]=[None], dp[1]=[tree(1)], dp[2]=[two trees], dp[3]=[5 trees]
examples.trace: Step 1: dp[0]=[None]. Step 2: i=1: as above, dp[1]=[tree(1)]. Step 3: i=2: as above, dp[2]=[tree(1,null,2), tree(2,1,null)]. Step 4: i=3: r=1: left_trees=dp[0]=[None], right_trees=dp[2]=[two trees], adjust right by adding 1, combine: two trees. r=2: left_trees=dp[1]=[tree(1)], right_trees=dp[1]=[tree(1)], adjust right by adding 2, combine: one tree. r=3: left_trees=dp[2]=[two trees], right_trees=dp[0]=[None], combine: two trees. Total 5 trees in dp[3].

testCases.expected: dp[0]=[None], dp[1]=[tree(1)], dp[2]=[two trees], dp[3]=[5 trees]
dp[0]=[None], dp[1]=[tree(1)], dp[2]=[2 trees], dp[3]=[5 trees], dp[4]=[14 trees]

ladders.title: Building Block — Clone and Offset a BST
ladders.desc: Create a deep copy of a BST and add an offset to all node values.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for handling right subtrees in the DP approach. When combining subtrees, the right subtree originally represents BSTs with values 1 to k (where k = i-r), but it needs to represent values r+1 to i. To achieve this, we clone the entire right subtree (to avoid modifying the original in dp) and add an offset r to every node value. The intuition is that BST structure is preserved under value translation: if a tree is a valid BST with values 1..k, adding a constant to all values maintains the BST property because the order relationships remain unchanged. This operation requires traversing the tree (e.g., using DFS or BFS) to create new nodes with adjusted values. For an empty tree (None), we return None. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of nodes in the tree, which in worst case is O(n). Space complexity is O(m) for the new tree. Connection to L2: This function is used within the combination subroutine to adjust right subtrees before attaching them. Common mistakes include shallow copying (leading to shared nodes), incorrect offset application, or not handling empty trees. Real code pattern: def clone_and_offset(node, offset): if not node: return None; new_node = TreeNode(node.val + offset); new_node.left = clone_and_offset(node.left, offset); new_node.right = clone_and_offset(node.right, offset); return new_node.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Create new node with value 1+2=3. Step 2: Left child is None, so new left is None. Step 3: Right child is node 2. Recursively clone: create new node with value 2+2=4, left=None, right=None. Step 4: Attach new right node 4 to root 3. Result: tree with root 3, left None, right 4.

Step 1: Root 2 becomes 3. Step 2: Left child 1 becomes 2, recursively: node 2 has no children, so tree(2). Step 3: Right child 3 becomes 4, recursively: tree(4). Step 4: Combine: root 3 with left 2 and right 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Creation and Traversal
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations for creating and traversing binary tree nodes.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic building blocks for working with binary trees, which are essential for implementing the DP approach. A tree node typically has attributes: value (or val), left child, and right child. Creation involves instantiating a node with a given value and setting left and right to None initially. Traversal is needed to clone trees or inspect structures; common methods include depth-first search (DFS) like pre-order, in-order, or post-order. For this problem, we use DFS for cloning: visit a node, create a new node with adjusted value, recursively process left and right subtrees, and link them. The intuition is that trees are recursive structures, so operations often use recursion or iteration with stacks. Time complexity for traversal is O(n) where n is nodes visited. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack height, where h is tree height. Connection to L3: This operation is used in the clone_and_offset function. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if base case is missed, incorrect linking of children, or mutable state issues. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. For traversal: def dfs(node): if not node: return; # process node; dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right).

examples.input: Create tree with root 1, left 2, right 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node with val=1. Step 2: Create left child node with val=2, set root.left to this node. Step 3: Create right child node with val=3, set root.right to this node. Step 4: Result is a tree where root 1 has left 2 and right 3.
Step 1: Start at root 1, visit node 1. Step 2: Go to left child: it's None, so return. Step 3: Go to right child: node 2, visit node 2. Step 4: Done.

testCases.input: Create tree: root=3, left=1, right=4, left.left=0
DFS to count nodes in tree(4,2,6,1,3,5,7)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree (BST) Properties
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic rules that define a binary search tree.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept required for the problem. A binary search tree (BST) is a binary tree where for every node: all values in the left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in the right subtree are greater than the node's value. This property ensures that in-order traversal yields values in sorted order. The intuition is that BSTs enable efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations, but here we focus on structure generation. Key points: nodes are typically labeled with unique values (e.g., integers 1 to n), the structure depends on insertion order, but for this problem, we consider all possible structures that satisfy the BST property with given values. For n nodes, the number of unique BSTs is given by the Catalan number C_n = (2n)!/((n+1)!*n!), which grows exponentially. This foundation explains why the problem is combinatorial and requires systematic generation. Connection to L4: This concept underpins why we create trees with specific value ranges and adjust them. Common mistakes include confusing BST with general binary trees, or misunderstanding the ordering property. Real code pattern: To check if a tree is BST: def is_bst(node, min_val, max_val): if not node: return True; if node.val <= min_val or node.val >= max_val: return False; return is_bst(node.left, min_val, node.val) and is_bst(node.right, node.val, max_val).

examples.trace: Step 1: Root is 2. Step 2: Left subtree has value 1, which is less than 2, okay. Step 3: Right subtree has value 3, which is greater than 2, okay. Step 4: Check recursively: left child 1 has no children, so valid; right child 3 has no children, so valid. All nodes satisfy BST property.

Step 1: Root is 1. Step 2: Left subtree is None, okay. Step 3: Right subtree is node 2, which is greater than 1, okay. Step 4: Node 2 has left=None and right=3. Right child 3 is greater than 2, okay. All conditions satisfied.

testCases.expected: Not BST (duplicate value or order issue)
